schools.summary: John's vague 'after these things' supplements the Synoptics; the better reading is anarthrous 'a feast,' so its identity stays uncertain (Lightfoot leans Passover).
Augustinian allegory: water=the Jewish people, five porches=the five books of Moses that convict but cannot cure, 38 years=the Law's defect made up by love's two commands.
Chrysostom: Jesus keeps the feast to show He is no enemy of the Law; the troubled pool is a lesser type of baptism and the Spirit's grace.
Calvin (from the chronology) and Gill weigh the three feasts; Calvin judges Pentecost.
Wesley: the feast is Pentecost.
Henry: Christ kept the feast as a subject under the law, teaching us to attend assemblies and seize gatherings for good.

debates.question: Which feast is meant?

sides.position: Pentecost
Passover
unknowable from the text

outliers.note: Reads the whole scene by numerology — five porches as the Pentateuch, 38/40 as the Law's deficiency supplied by the two precepts of love.

schools.summary: Read 'sheep-gate' (Nehemiah 3:1), not 'sheep-market'; 'pool' is a rare swimming-bath word; Bethesda = 'house of mercy'; the present 'there is' may date the Gospel before AD 70.
Calvin: the famous, crowded site near the temple proves the miracle was public; he rejects Jerome's re-spelling of the name.
Wesley/Clarke: 'there is' shows John wrote before the destruction; Clarke notes a variant in the name and the pool's later survival.

debates.question: Does the present 'there is' fix the Gospel's date before Jerusalem's fall?

sides.position: yes, evidence of a pre-70 date
only John's vivid memory; the site survived anyway

outliers.note: Takes 'in the Hebrew tongue' to mean the Chaldee/Aramaic vernacular adopted after the Babylonian return, not classical Hebrew.

schools.summary: Plain sense of the blind/lame/withered multitude; the closing clause 'waiting for the moving of the water' is judged a later gloss by the oldest MSS and modern editors.
Gill reads the impotent as fallen men under the law of works; Calvin restrains the wonder, noting begging and a fixed number cured to commend temple worship.
Clarke admits the manuscript doubt but keeps the disputed clause as authentic.

debates.question: Is 'waiting for the moving of the water' (with v.4) original or a later gloss?

sides.position: a non-original gloss explaining v.7
authentic; the evidence is insufficient to delete it

schools.summary: Robertson: the whole verse is absent from the oldest MSS and added to clarify v.7; Barnes mediates — the angel was unseen and the spring may have had natural medicinal properties.
Calvin: God uses angelic ministry to display His own power (Colossians 1:16), not from need; it is wicked to credit the creature.
Wesley grants many thought it natural; the periodic descent revived hope that God had not forgotten His people. Clarke keeps 'angel of the Lord' as genuine.

debates.question: Is the verse authentic, and was the agency truly angelic or natural?

sides.position: a non-original addition; possibly natural medicinal water
genuine and supernaturally angelic

schools.summary: The thirty-eight years marks the duration of the disease, not age or time at the pool; the long, incurable case magnifies the miracle, and this man alone is addressed first and unasked.
Calvin: the Evangelist heaps up circumstances — hopeless duration, no helper, failed attempts — to prove the miracle certain and exalt Christ's power.
Clarke: probably palsy (Chrysostom guessed blindness); poverty and disease joined; the length rules out collusion.
Robertson: 'knew' may be supernatural, observed, or overheard; Lightfoot hears a deliberate sabbath edge in the question; Ellicott foreshadows the man's personal sin (v.14).
Calvin: the question kindles desire and rouses the witnesses so the sudden cure is not missed.
Clarke: such addresses are always proper from preachers, to stir sinners to desire and expect salvation.
Chrysostom opens on the all-sufficient medicinal profit of the Scriptures themselves (Rom 15:4; 1 Cor 10:11).

outliers.note: Reads the man's infirmity as the mark of his own past sin, anticipating Christ's warning in v.14.

schools.summary: The reply is helplessness not refusal; the popular belief gave virtue to the first one into the troubled water; Ellicott reads 'an eloquence of helplessness.'
Calvin: the man limits God's help to his own narrow conception, a mirror of our weakness, which Christ graciously forgives.
Wesley: he gives the reason he was not whole; Clarke notes the variant 'Yea, Lord' and the misery of poverty joined to disease.

Robertson parses the three imperatives and the 'pallet'; Lightfoot sets the rabbinic private/public sabbath rule; Barnes: carrying the bed proved the cure real. Clarke: Jesus commands with authority belonging to God alone.
Cyril of Jerusalem: 'Jesus' = Saviour/Healer; He heals the soul first ('sin no more') then the body, palsied for the soul's sin.
Clarke: the instant, total cure is incontestable proof of Christ's omnipotence.

outliers.note: Etymologizes 'Jesus' as Saviour/Healer and orders the cure soul-before-body.

schools.summary: Robertson: precise tenses and the first of Jesus' Jerusalem sabbath violations (John 9:14,16); Barnes: the Son of man as Lord of the sabbath may dispense with the rule (Matthew 12:8).
Calvin: Christ deliberately courted the offense to publicize the miracle and open the way for the following discourse.
Carrying burdens was unlawful on the sabbath (Ex 23:12; Neh 13:19; Jer 17:21), the penalty stoning; the healing was 'a minor detail' to them, who cavil at what they cannot deny.
Calvin: they justly accuse the man for the sabbath but become at fault when his explanation is rejected; Christ who laid on the burden discharges him by His own authority.
Henry: the eased sinner is in danger of returning once terror lifts, unless grace dries up the fountain (anticipating v.14).
The man reasons conclusively: power to heal carries the right to command, and mercy received imposes obedience; he obeys though ignorant even of Jesus' name.
Clarke: a worker of such a miracle must be a good man, who commands no evil — so the bed-carrying cannot be wrong.
Unanimous: the contemptuous question ('Who is the fellow?') fastens on the command, omits the miracle, and aims at an indictment of Christ.
Cyril of Jerusalem alone lifts the eye above the cavil, naming Jesus the Healer of soul and body.

Robertson on the rare verb 'to slip out'; Ellicott corrects the reading — Jesus disappeared among the existing crowd, not that a crowd gathered; Clarke: He withdrew to avoid the rulers, His work unfinished.
Calvin: Christ withdrew so the fact could be judged on its own; the cure cannot be ascribed to the man's faith, since he still does not know his Healer.

debates.question: Does the clause mean a crowd had gathered, or that Jesus slipped away through the existing multitude?

sides.position: Jesus disappeared among the multitude already present
a crowd had assembled at the scene

themes: cure not grounded in the man's faith

schools.summary: Chrysostom: sin is the soul's ruin, and God often punishes the body for the soul's transgressions; the warning shows the disease was the wage of sin.

Robertson: 'no longer go on sinning' implies disease from personal sin, but not all sickness is so (John 9:3); Barnes/Ellicott stress thanksgiving in the temple and freedom from the cause, not just the effect.
The Catena: the healed man seeks the temple, not pleasure, and knows Jesus in the sacred place though not in the crowd; Alcuin — to see our Maker we must avoid the crowd.
Calvin: affliction is divine discipline; we misuse God's gifts if recovery does not issue in obedience.
Wesley: the former illness was the effect or punishment of sin.

debates.question: How far does the sin-sickness link reach?

sides.position: a real connection of sin and suffering
real here, but not a universal rule

schools.summary: Robertson reads it as betrayal — the man ignored the warning and told the Jews, ungrateful or self-protective; Ellicott more neutrally sees only the answer he could not give at v.13.
Calvin: the man's intent was pious, to honour his Physician, never to expose Christ to hatred.
Clarke: he reported the cure, not the command, leaving the inference; Wesley turns the indictment on the hostile Jews.

debates.question: Was the healed man treacherous or well-meaning in telling the Jews?

sides.position: ungrateful, even a betrayal
a pious, well-meant act of honour

schools.summary: Imperfect tenses ('began to persecute,' 'was doing') make the charge a settled pattern of sabbath-breaking, the occasion for an open breach; the clause 'and sought to slay him' is likely inserted from v.18.
Barnes: the bigot assumes guilt rather than inquiring whether Christ had proved Himself the Messiah.

debates.question: Is 'and sought to slay him' original?

sides.position: a later insertion from v.18
keep it; true of the Jews regardless

schools.summary: 'My Father' (not 'our') claims a peculiar relation; the present tense means ceaseless work without a sabbath break (so Philo, Justin, Origen); God rested from creating but never from governing, so the sabbath does not bind Him.
Calvin: Christ does not call the sabbath abolished but denies any violation, since this is a divine work — though the ceremonial sabbath was a shadow ended by His coming.
Wesley: God works without intermission from creation; the verse is the proposition expounded in vv.19-30.
Cyril of Jerusalem worships the Maker as Father of Christ against heretics who sever the Old Testament from the New; Lightfoot: Christ asserts the Messiah is God.

themes: sabbath does not bind the Creator

schools.summary: 'His own Father' is a unique, unparalleled claim to divinity; the imperfect 'sought the more' pictures rising, untiring murderous intent (John 7:1; 8:37).
Wesley: 'his own Father' in a sense no creature can use; the hearers so understood, and Christ confirmed rather than denied it. Clarke: two pretexts now — sabbath-breaking and blasphemy.
Calvin (with Barnes): Christ refused to soften the claim, teaching us never to yield to the fury of the wicked but to maintain God's truth.
Calvin: Christ does not deny but affirms His equality; the words mean the inseparable working of Father and Son, not inferiority — Arius wrongly pressed the text against the Son.
Hilary: what the Son does is the Father's doing acting in Him, asserting the excellency of His nature; Augustine: the operation of Father and Son is inseparable (John 1:3).

Robertson: the absolute 'the Son' admits and defends the equality of v.18; Clarke argues from mutual dependence that one who does whatsoever God does is no creature; Ellicott: the Father-Son relation is the key both Jews and moderns miss.
Wesley: the inability is not imperfection but glory, flowing from eternal, indissoluble unity with the Father.
Cyril of Jerusalem: the Son's dependence is His honouring of the Father; Lightfoot reads 'the Son' as the sent Messiah-Servant (Isaiah 42:1).

debates.question: Does 'can do nothing of himself' prove the Son's inferiority?

sides.position: no — it expresses inseparable operation and equality (anti-Arian)
the Arian misreading of subordination, rejected by all

themes: inseparable operation of Father and Son

schools.summary: The Father conceals nothing from the Son; the relation is reciprocal (oneness of essence); 'greater works' = raising the dead and judgment; Robertson declines to force the two Greek love-verbs apart.
Calvin rejects the fathers' 'God loves himself in the Son' as far-fetched, applying the love to Christ clothed with flesh, the beloved (Matthew 3:17).
Wesley: Father shows-and-does while Son sees-and-does at the same time — intimate unity; Clarke: Christ speaks now as God, now as ambassador.
Augustine keeps inseparable operation — the Father works all through the Son as His Word (John 1:3).

outliers.note: Notes the two Greek verbs for love used of the Father-Son bond but refuses a sharp distinction here.

schools.summary: Augustine: the Son raises the same dead as the Father, by the same will — not Father some, Son others; these are the 'greater works.'

Ellicott frames vv.21-29 as resurrection/judgment both spiritual and physical, present and future; Barnes/Clarke cite OT raisings (1 Kgs 17:22; 2 Kgs 4) the Son will match (Nain, Lazarus); Robertson: a claim on a par with the Father before any raising.
Calvin: the Son is the Author of life — righteousness and all the Spirit's gifts; the 'whom he will' is the Son's free life-giving choice, limited only by receptivity (Ellicott).

debates.question: Is the raising spiritual or physical?

sides.position: both — spiritual now, physical at the last day

schools.summary: Committed judgment crowns the equality argument (sabbath, works, raising) and implies omniscience; the perfect 'hath given' is a settled state (Matthew 25:31-46); their acts are common, their power equal.
Calvin: the Father governs the world in the person of the Son, giving Him a kingdom to rule heaven and earth, exercising His own authority through Him.
Wesley: the Father judges by the ordained man (Acts 17:31); Cyril of Jerusalem turns judgment to terror — conscience will witness against the secrets of men.

themes: all judgment given to the Son
governing the world through the Son

schools.summary: Clarke/Wesley: equal honour proves equal deity — to worship a creature is idolatry; were He God only by office He could not be honoured with the same honour as the Father.
Barnes/Robertson: Christ claims the same right to worship as the Father; dishonouring the Son dishonours the sending Father (John 8:49; 1 John 2:23).
Calvin: God is present and worshipped in the Son, so we must seek the Father in Christ.
Chrysostom sets the account at the tribunal (2 Cor 5:10); Cyril grounds the honour in the mutual knowledge of Father and Son — the begotten is God.

'Heareth' = receive and obey; the perfect 'hath passed out of death into life' is a completed present transfer, the spiritual life distinct from v.28; 'doth not come into judgment' (present), limited only by receptivity.
Augustine: faith is the step, understanding the fruit; why hear Christ yet believe the Father — because of their unity.
Calvin: the gospel is Christ's scepter; against false profession, the hearing meant is real subjection.
Wesley's distinctive: the believer comes not into condemnation 'unless he make shipwreck of the faith' — a conditional note.

debates.question: Is the believer's security unconditional?

sides.position: a present, settled passage from death to life
conditional — 'unless he make shipwreck of the faith'

outliers.note: Adds the Arminian proviso 'unless he make shipwreck of the faith' to the assurance of no condemnation.

schools.summary: The decisive 'and now is' makes this the present spiritual resurrection (not v.28's bodily one); the dead are the dead in trespasses (Eph 2:1; 5:14), raised in conversion.
Augustine takes 'now is' of the soul's resurrection; Chrysostom adds that the phrase shows how near it stands.
Calvin: Christ's repeated oath secures the incredible truth that the Son's very voice imparts life.
Wesley illustrates with the bodily raisings (Jairus, the widow's son, Lazarus); Lightfoot notes the Jews' expectation of resurrection at Messiah's coming (Ezek 37; Isa 26:19).

debates.question: Does 'the dead shall hear... and now is' mean spiritual or bodily resurrection?

sides.position: present spiritual resurrection
illustrated by the literal raisings

schools.summary: 'In himself' = underived life, unlike our derived life (Gen 2:7); the Living God against idols (Acts 14:15); 'as... so' marks the fact not the degree (Westcott).
Wesley: life given by eternal generation, absolute and independent; Clarke refers the 'given' to His character as Messiah/envoy.
Calvin: the Son is the fountain holding life's fullness and communicating it to all (Psalm 36:9).
Cyril of Jerusalem: the gift of self-life proves the begotten Son is God.

debates.question: When is life 'given' to the Son?

sides.position: by eternal generation
to the pre-existent Son naturally, given to the incarnate

schools.summary: Ellicott/Robertson read anarthrous 'a son of man' stressing the humanity needed to judge men; Lightfoot/Clarke read the Messianic title from Daniel 7:13; Clarke notes the alternative punctuation joining to v.28.
The Catena: the Father gave the Son power both to give life and to judge; Chrysostom: judgment/resurrection/life are the most persuasive arguments to faith.
Calvin: 'authority' = rule and government, the Father appointing the Son King over all.
Wesley: appointed to judge mankind because made man; Cyril links the Son of man to the willing passion.

debates.question: Does 'the Son of man' here stress His humanity or His Messianic dignity?

sides.position: anarthrous 'a son of man' — the humanity that fits Him to judge men
the Messianic title of Daniel 7:13

schools.summary: The absence of 'and now is' (unlike v.25) makes this the bodily resurrection of all in the graves at the last day; Robertson on the word for sepulchre; Barnes: God can regather the dust, not necessarily the same particles.
Calvin: Christ argues from the bodily resurrection not as the greater power but because carnal minds admire only the visible.

debates.question: Punctuation: does 'because he is the Son of man' belong with v.27 or with 'Marvel not'?

sides.position: join it to 'Marvel not' (Syriac, Chrysostom, Theophylact)
keep it closing v.27 (the common reading)

schools.summary: Prefer 'judgment' to 'damnation'; the rare words for 'done good'/'practised evil' form a double opposition (John 3:20-21); the phrases are unique (cf. Luke 14:14); the good-doers are Christ's friends (Mt 25:34-36).
Calvin guards against works-righteousness: good works mark believers as fruit (Mt 7:16), shown by the reborn dying robber (Luke 23:42); fruit, not purchase.
Wesley: 'the resurrection of life' is the resurrection that leads to life everlasting.

'As I hear, I judge' is the speech of one commissioned/taught (John 8:28); the judgment is 'righteous' because guided by the Father's will; Lightfoot's parallel of the inferior judge consulting the Sanhedrin.
The Catena: the Son can wish nothing unlike the Father; 'as I hear, I judge' = 'as if the Father judged.'
Calvin: needless to wrangle over eternal Divinity here; the ancients' anxiety over Arius was excessive — the Son justly claims equality.
Wesley: impossible to act separately from the Father, being essentially united; Clarke locates the subjection in the assumed human nature (John 1:14).

themes: human nature subject to the Deity

schools.summary: Chrysostom: 'true' refers not to the testimony's intrinsic value but to its standing as legal proof; otherwise our hope would fail.

A man's witness in his own case was inadmissible (Deut 19:15; Mt 18:16), so 'not true' = not legally credited; reconciled with John 8:14 by this legal sense; Clarke reports Pearce's interrogative reading.
Calvin: spoken by concession — 'let it go for nothing' by your custom — without really lessening its credit; Wesley: 'if I alone (impossible)' it is not valid.
Henry: the self-witness is infallibly true (John 8:14) but men's rule of judgment will not admit it.

debates.question: How is this reconciled with 'my witness is true' (John 8:14)?

sides.position: a concession to legal custom; 'true' = legally admissible
read interrogatively, 'is not my witness true?'

schools.summary: The 'another' is the Father, not the Baptist (named in v.33) — the continual witness resumed in vv.36-38 (John 8:17); excluded as the Baptist by what follows.
Gill dissents: the 'another' is 'another man,' John the Baptist, since the Father is distinctly named in v.37 and the passage builds by gradation.

debates.question: Who is the 'another' that bears witness?

sides.position: the Father
John the Baptist

schools.summary: The perfects give John's witness abiding value the Sanhedrin must own; 'ye' opposed to 'I'; John's mission was to witness to the Light (John 1:7,19); his character made the testimony unexceptionable.
Calvin presses it ad hominem: having owned John as a prophet, to reject his words convicts the Jews of their own malice.
Wesley: John testified 'that I am the Christ.'
Christ does not rest on a man's witness (the 'I' against the 'ye'); He received John's witness only to lead them to Himself, 'evidence of their own seeking,' offered that they might be saved.
Calvin: Christ uses John's testimony not from need but for our confirmation, as the disciples would later witness (Acts 1:8).
Wesley: He seriously willed their salvation, yet most died in their sins; Cyril: receive no testimony from man apart from Scripture.

The word is 'lamp' (luchnos), not the sun (to phos): John was the kindled lamp shining till the Sun arose (John 1:8); the past 'was' implies he is now imprisoned; Lightfoot's Jewish 'candle of the law.'
Calvin: God kindled the lamp before their eyes, proving their blindness voluntary; 'for a season' exposes their fickleness.
Wesley: inwardly burning with zeal, outwardly shining in holiness; their willingness lasted only a short time.

themes: John the lamp not the Light
kindled lamp till the Sun arose

schools.summary: Greater than John's witness, not John himself; the works (not only miracles) bear witness the Father sent Him; 'to finish' (cf. John 4:34) looks to 'It is finished' (John 19:30).
Calvin: having exposed how the Jews corrupted John's witness, Christ sends them to the Father, whose works attest His Sonship; Clarke: not obliged to depend on John alone.
Calvin: the perfect tense shows a long-standing witness given whenever God spoke in the Law and Prophets, not one event; Robertson too excludes the baptism/transfiguration voices as the reference.
Robertson: the direct witness of the Father beyond the works; Ellicott on the imagery of voice and shape; Barnes lists miracles, baptism, prophecies and suspects 'all.'
Wesley fixes on the baptism voice (Mt 3:17); Clarke: by the prophets, the 'voice/shape' clause a parenthesis (Pearce), with the lately-added heavenly voice.

debates.question: What is the Father's witness here?

sides.position: the whole testimony of the Law and Prophets
the heavenly voice at the baptism
all of these together

themes: never heard His voice nor seen His shape

schools.summary: The word 'abiding' is a Johannine image of indwelling (John 15:7; 1 John 2:14); they locked Scripture in ark and synagogue but gave it no home; rejecting the Sent One proves the word does not abide.
Calvin: the word has no place because they reject the Son, on whom Moses and the prophets everywhere bid them come.
Clarke: the word in the mouth but not the mind, unlike the Psalmist (Ps 119:11); Wesley: all who believe have the word abiding in them.

Lean indicative ('Ye search'): Lightfoot, Clarke, Gill, and Robertson (the indicative 'because ye think' decides it); Barnes keeps the imperative but stresses the verb's force — to search as miners for ore (Job 28:3).
Wesley reads it imperatively — 'a plain command to all men'; the Scriptures show the way to eternal life and testify of Christ.
Henry holds both: they do search, and do well, yet may be studious in the letter while strangers to its power.
Calvin: not blamed for seeking life in Scripture, but for resting in the dead letter while missing the Christ it testifies of.

debates.question: Is 'Search the scriptures' imperative or indicative?

sides.position: indicative — 'Ye search the Scriptures'
imperative — a command to search
both senses held together

outliers.note: Threads the witness of John and the prophets to the Word, somewhat apart from the imperative/indicative question.

themes: the dead letter vs the living Word

schools.summary: The refusal is of the will, moral not intellectual (Ellicott); Robertson: 'the tragedy of the rejection of Messiah by the Messianic race' (John 1:11; Mt 23:37; 3:19); life is the Gospel's aim (20:31).
Calvin: nothing but their own malice hinders them; willfully blind, fleeing the light and seeking to extinguish the sun by unbelief.
Chrysostom: Christ guards against the suspicion of seeking human glory — His nature does not want it.
Not wounded pride but a setting-aside of self-interest; unlike the Jews who loved men's praise (5:44; 12:43; cf. 1 Thess 2:6); 'glory' the better rendering, beginning the diagnosis of their unbelief.
Calvin: Christ is too great to depend on men's opinions; the world's malignity can take nothing from Him.

The perfect 'I have come to know' is knowledge of personal experience (John 2:24); 'love of God' is an objective genitive (love toward God, Luke 11:42); they wore the first precept (Deut 6:4-9) on phylacteries (Mt 23:5) but lacked it within.
Clarke: their opposition is not zeal for God but incorrigible ignorance and malicious jealousy; Wesley: the piercing word spoken with tenderest compassion.
Calvin: the love of God is the Law's summary (Deut 6:5); its absence leaves no desire to obey.

Deliberate antithesis: Christ comes in the Father's name (the 'Name' frequent in John, 17:6,11); 'another in his own name' read as false teachers/messiahs (Barnes, Clarke citing Josephus) or the antichrist (Robertson).
Cyril of Jerusalem: prophecy fulfilled — the Jews rejected the true Messiah and await the deceiver, so 'him ye will receive.'
Calvin: false prophets (and the Pope) boast the title, but Christ means the reality, proving His commission by faithfully fulfilling it.

debates.question: Who is the 'another' that comes in his own name?

sides.position: false messiahs/teachers in history
the eschatological Antichrist

themes: rejecting the true, receiving the false

schools.summary: The emphatic 'ye' makes belief impossible in this disposition — to believe would cost their whole present life; not true Jews who sought God's glory (Rom 2:29); they expected a Messiah of pomp.
Clarke: pride, vanity, and self-love the grand obstacle — they lived on mutual praise and chose reputation over their souls; Wesley adds the mortifying Pentecost setting of self-praise.
Calvin: ambition has robbed the proud priests and scribes of sound judgment.

themes: love of human honour bars faith

schools.summary: Legal vocabulary of accusation in court; the words cut deep yet love cannot accuse — Christ judges only as light reveals darkness (John 3:19); Moses, who wrote of Messiah, becomes their accuser.
Calvin: the hardened, when instruction fails, must be summoned to God's judgment-seat, against those who flatter themselves with a gracious God they oppose.
Henry: many trust forms, doctrines, or parties without entering their meaning, as the Jews did not enter Moses'.
Wesley: Moses accuses them by his writings.

A second-class (unfulfilled) condition — a home-thrust proving they never really believed Moses, who wrote of Christ (Deut 18:18; Gen 49:10; Gen 22:18; the serpent, John 3:14; Abraham, John 8:56); Thorah means 'instruction,' a manifestation of God they rested above.
Calvin: ministers must defend the word against despisers; Moses' doctrine, outwardly owned, is inwardly rejected.
Wesley: Moses wrote of Christ everywhere, in all his writings.

themes: unbelief in Moses = unbelief in Christ

schools.summary: The logic parallels Luke 16:31; Robertson contrasts permanent 'writings' with spoken 'words' (Moses' authority supreme for Jews), while Ellicott stresses 'his' vs 'My' — either way no ground of hope is left; Barnes: many profess the Bible yet disregard it when crossed.
Calvin: Christ claims seemingly less authority than Moses only by accommodation to hearers who held the Law sacred; the contrast aggravates their unbelief.

debates.question: Where does the contrast lie?

sides.position: between 'writings' and 'words'
between 'his' and 'My'
